walkthrough.title: Place the Galactic Center Sector
walkthrough.text: Let's get Eclipse set up. First, find the Galactic Center Sector tile — it is the unique hex labeled 001. Place it in the center of the table. Then take the Galactic Center Defense System miniature, the big imposing ship, and place it on the sector. Finally, draw one Discovery Tile from the face-down stack, and place it face-down underneath the GCDS miniature on the Galactic Center Sector.
walkthrough.image_caption: Galactic Center Sector with GCDS miniature

walkthrough.title: Prepare the Sector Stacks
walkthrough.text: Sort all the hex Sector tiles by their ring number — the Roman numeral on the back. Inner Sectors have a I, Middle Sectors have a II, and Outer Sectors have a III. Shuffle each ring separately into its own face-down stack. The number of Outer Sectors you use depends on player count: 5 for 2 players, 8 for 3, 14 for 4, 16 for 5, and 18 for 6. If you are not using the optional Warp Portals module, remove Sectors 281, 381, and 382 before shuffling. Place the three stacks near the Galactic Center.
walkthrough.image_caption: Three Sector stacks sorted by ring

walkthrough.title: Fill the Tech Tray
walkthrough.text: Put all 114 Tech Tiles into the white Tech Tile Bag and shake it up. Draw tiles randomly until you have the correct number of regular Tech Tiles for your player count: 12 for 2 players, 14 for 3, 16 for 4, 18 for 5, or 20 for 6. Place each drawn tile in its matching slot on the Tech Tray according to category — Military, Grid, or Nano — and cost. Stack duplicates in the same slot. Any Rare Tech Tiles drawn go in any of the seven bottom-row slots and do not count toward the regular tile number.
walkthrough.image_caption: Tech Tray filled with Tech Tiles by category and cost

walkthrough.title: Set Up the Upgrade Tray and Discovery Tiles
walkthrough.text: Arrange all 282 Ship Part Tiles by type in the Upgrade Tray — this is the long tray with labeled compartments for each ship part. Next, shuffle all the Discovery Tiles and place them in a face-down stack near the Tech Tray and Upgrade Tray. Also place the 12 purple Damage Cubes, the Traitor Tile, and the 24 custom dice nearby.
walkthrough.image_caption: Upgrade Tray with sorted Ship Part Tiles

walkthrough.title: Prepare the Reputation Tiles
walkthrough.text: Put all 33 Reputation Tiles into the black Reputation Tile Bag and give it a good shake. These tiles are drawn during combat and are worth 1 to 4 victory points each. Keep the bag within reach of all players.
walkthrough.image_caption: Reputation Tile Bag ready for draws

walkthrough.title: Choose Blueprint Tiles
walkthrough.text: There are three types of Blueprint Tiles: Ancient, Guardian, and GCDS. Choose one of each type to use for this game. Tiles with two notches on their border are advanced variants — only use those with experienced players. Place the chosen Blueprint Tiles near the Upgrade Tray where everyone can reference them. These show the stats for the non-player ships you will fight during the game.
walkthrough.image_caption: Blueprint Tiles for Ancients, Guardians, and GCDS

walkthrough.title: Choose Species and Starting Sectors
walkthrough.text: Give the Start Player Tile to the player who has spent the least time on planet Earth. Beginning with the player to the right of the Start Player and going counterclockwise, each player chooses a Species Board — the double-sided board showing your civilization's abilities, tech tracks, and ship blueprints. Take the matching-color Species Tray containing your 18 Ship miniatures, 33 Population Cubes, 16 Influence Discs, and 3 Ambassador Tiles. Place your Starting Sector hex in the Starting Zone closest to you, oriented so the arrow faces the GCDS.
walkthrough.image_caption: Species Board with matching tray components

walkthrough.title: Fill Guardian Sectors
walkthrough.text: Fill any empty Starting Zones with random Guardian Sectors, oriented so their arrows face the GCDS. In a 6-player game there are no Guardian Sectors. Place a face-down Discovery Tile on the Guardian Symbol of each Guardian Sector, then place a Guardian miniature on top. Put any unused Starting Sectors, Guardian Sectors, and Species Boards back in the box.
walkthrough.image_caption: Guardian Sectors placed in empty starting zones

walkthrough.title: Set Up Your Control Board
walkthrough.text: Assemble your Control Board by placing the punchboard top onto the card-stock bottom. Place one Population Cube on each square valued 3 through 28 on all three Population Tracks: Materials, Science, and Money. Then place one Storage Marker of each color — orange for Materials, pink for Science, brown for Money — on your Storage Track at the starting values shown on your Species Board.
walkthrough.image_caption: Control Board with Population Cubes on tracks

walkthrough.title: Place Influence Discs
walkthrough.text: Take your 16 Influence Discs from the Species Tray and place 13 of them on the Influence Track of your Species Board, filling from the right side. Leave the three extra discs in your tray — these are only used if you research Advanced Robotics or Quantum Grid later. The rightmost empty space on the Influence Track shows your current Civilization Upkeep Cost in Money.
walkthrough.image_caption: Influence Track with 13 discs placed

walkthrough.title: Set Out Colony Ships and Summary Tile
walkthrough.text: Take the number of Colony Ship tiles shown on your Species Board — typically 3 or 4 — and place them face-up next to your board. Also take your Summary Tile and place it with the Action Phase Overview face-up. Keep your Ship miniatures and Ambassador Tiles in your Species Tray for easy access.
walkthrough.image_caption: Colony Ships and Summary Tile beside Species Board

walkthrough.title: Populate Your Starting Sector
walkthrough.text: Place one Interceptor Ship miniature on your Starting Sector (the Orion Hegemony places a Cruiser instead). For each Population Square on your Starting Sector that does not have a star symbol, move one Population Cube from the matching Population Track on your Control Board to that square. Finally, take one Influence Disc from the leftmost space of your Influence Track and place it on the Influence Space of your Starting Sector. You now Control this sector and are ready to play.
walkthrough.image_caption: Starting Sector with ship, population, and influence disc

summary.title: Place the Galactic Center Sector
bullets: Place Sector 001 in the center of the table
Place the GCDS miniature on it
Place 1 face-down Discovery Tile underneath the GCDS

summary.title: Prepare the Sector Stacks
bullets: Sort Sectors by ring: Inner (I), Middle (II), Outer (III)
Shuffle each ring separately into face-down stacks
Outer stack size by player count: 2p=5, 3p=8, 4p=14, 5p=16, 6p=18
Remove Sectors 281, 381-382 if not using Warp Portals

summary.title: Fill the Tech Tray
bullets: Draw from white Tech Tile Bag: 2p=12, 3p=14, 4p=16, 5p=18, 6p=20 regular tiles
Place tiles in Tech Tray by category (Military/Grid/Nano) and cost
Rare Techs go in bottom row, do not count toward draw total

summary.title: Set Up the Upgrade Tray and Discovery Tiles
bullets: Sort all 282 Ship Part Tiles by type in the Upgrade Tray
Shuffle Discovery Tiles into a face-down stack
Place 12 purple Damage Cubes, Traitor Tile, and 24 dice nearby

summary.title: Prepare the Reputation Tiles
bullets: Place all 33 Reputation Tiles in the black bag
Tiles are drawn during combat, worth 1-4 VP each

summary.title: Choose Blueprint Tiles
bullets: Select 1 Ancient, 1 Guardian, 1 GCDS Blueprint Tile
Two-notch border = advanced variant (experienced players only)
These define non-player ship stats for the game

summary.title: Choose Species and Starting Sectors
bullets: Start Player = least time on planet Earth; pick species counterclockwise from their right
Each player takes Species Board + matching Species Tray (18 Ships, 33 Cubes, 16 Discs, 3 Ambassadors)
Place your Starting Sector in the nearest Starting Zone, arrow facing GCDS

summary.title: Fill Guardian Sectors
bullets: Fill empty Starting Zones with Guardian Sectors (arrow toward GCDS)
Place 1 face-down Discovery Tile + 1 Guardian miniature on each
No Guardian Sectors in a 6-player game

summary.title: Set Up Your Control Board
bullets: Assemble Control Board (punchboard top on card-stock bottom)
Place 1 Population Cube on each square from 3-28 on all three tracks
Place 3 Storage Markers at your Species Board's starting values

summary.title: Place Influence Discs
bullets: Place 13 Influence Discs on the Influence Track (fill from right)
Leave 3 extra discs in tray (used only with Advanced Robotics / Quantum Grid)
Rightmost empty space = your Civilization Upkeep Cost

summary.title: Set Out Colony Ships and Summary Tile
bullets: Take Colony Ships shown on Species Board (typically 3-4), place face-up
Place Summary Tile with Action Phase Overview face-up
Keep Ships and Ambassador Tiles in Species Tray

summary.title: Populate Your Starting Sector
bullets: Place 1 Interceptor on Starting Sector (Orion Hegemony: Cruiser)
Move Population Cubes to non-star Population Squares from matching tracks
Place leftmost Influence Disc on the Sector's Influence Space — you Control it

walkthrough.title: Round Structure
walkthrough.text: Eclipse is played over eight rounds. Each round has four phases in order: Action Phase, Combat Phase, Upkeep Phase, and Cleanup Phase. During the Action Phase, players take turns clockwise starting from the Start Player. On your turn you take one Action or Pass. This continues until all players have Passed. Then you resolve combat, pay upkeep costs, produce resources, and prepare for the next round.
walkthrough.image_caption: Round structure overview

walkthrough.title: Taking Actions and the Influence Cost
walkthrough.text: Every time you take an Action, you must move the leftmost Influence Disc from your Influence Track to the corresponding Action Space on your Action Track. This is critical — the more actions you take and sectors you control, the more money you need to pay during Upkeep. Each action literally costs you upkeep capacity. The number of actions you can take is only limited by the number of Influence Discs on your Influence Track. You can stack multiple discs on the same action space to take the same action multiple times across different turns.
walkthrough.image_caption: Moving Influence Discs from track to action spaces

walkthrough.title: Action: Explore
walkthrough.text: The Explore Action lets you discover new sectors. For each Explore Activation, choose an unexplored zone adjacent to a sector where you have Control or an Unpinned Ship. Flip a sector tile from the matching ring stack. You may either place it — orienting it so a Wormhole Connection exists with your sector — or discard it. If the placed sector has a Discovery Tile symbol, place a face-down Discovery Tile on it. If it also has Ancient symbols, place that many Ancient miniatures on the tile. You may take the Discovery Tile from undefended sectors immediately. After placing, you may take Control by placing an Influence Disc from your track onto the sector's Influence Space. You may also use Colony Ships to populate the new sector.
walkthrough.image_caption: Exploring and placing a new Sector tile

walkthrough.title: Action: Research
walkthrough.text: The Research Action lets you acquire new Technologies. For each Research Activation, choose a Tech Tile from the Tech Tray and pay its Science cost. Place it on the leftmost available space of its category track — Military, Grid, or Nano — on your Species Board. Rare Techs can go on any track. Each tech you already have in a category gives a discount on future techs in that same category, but every tech has a minimum cost. You cannot research a tech you already own, and you cannot discard researched techs. Techs unlock Ship Parts for Upgrading, allow Building Starbases and Structures, or provide instant or permanent abilities.
walkthrough.image_caption: Researching a Tech Tile from the Tech Tray

walkthrough.title: Action: Upgrade
walkthrough.text: The Upgrade Action lets you modify your Ship Blueprints. For each Upgrade Activation you may first return any number of Ship Part Tiles from your blueprints to the Upgrade Tray, then place one Ship Part Tile from the Upgrade Tray onto an empty or pre-printed space on any blueprint. You must have researched the required tech for that ship part, and the ship's total Energy Consumption must not exceed its Energy Production. Drive tiles cannot go on Starbases, and at least one Drive must remain on Interceptor, Cruiser, and Dreadnought blueprints. When you upgrade a blueprint, all ships of that type are instantly upgraded everywhere on the board.
walkthrough.image_caption: Upgrading Ship Blueprints with new parts

walkthrough.title: Action: Build
walkthrough.text: The Build Action lets you construct Ships and Structures. For each Build Activation, pay the Materials cost shown on your Species Board and place the ship or structure in any sector you Control. Basic costs are: Interceptor 3, Cruiser 5, Dreadnought 8, Starbase 3, Orbital 4, Monolith 10. Starbases require the Starbase tech, Orbitals require the Orbital tech, and Monoliths require the Monolith tech. Each sector may have at most one Monolith and one Orbital. Orbitals provide an extra Population Square for Science or Money.
walkthrough.image_caption: Building Ships and Structures

walkthrough.title: Action: Move
walkthrough.text: The Move Action lets you reposition your Ships. For each Move Activation, move one ship up to a number of sectors equal to its Movement Value — the total Drive value on its blueprint. Ships can only move through Wormhole Connections between sectors. Ships cannot move to unexplored zones. Pinned ships cannot leave — each opponent ship in a sector pins one of your ships. The GCDS pins all ships in the Galactic Center Sector. Moving your ships into a sector where another player has ships or control can trigger combat in the Combat Phase and may break Diplomatic Relations.
walkthrough.image_caption: Moving Ships through Wormhole Connections

walkthrough.title: Action: Influence
walkthrough.text: The Influence Action lets you rearrange Control. For each Influence Activation, move an Influence Disc from your Influence Track or a controlled sector to an uncontrolled sector with a Wormhole Connection where no opponent ships are present, or to a sector where only you have ships, or back to your Influence Track. You may not use more than one Influence Activation in the same sector per action. Removing a disc from a sector returns all your Population Cubes there to their respective tracks. The Influence Action also lets you flip up to two used Colony Ships face-up.
walkthrough.image_caption: Using Influence to rearrange sector control

walkthrough.title: Passing and Reactions
walkthrough.text: The first time you Pass in a round, flip your Summary Tile to the Reactions Overview side. The first player to Pass receives 2 Money and the Start Player Tile for the next round. After Passing, on subsequent turns you may take a Reaction instead of passing again. Reactions are weaker single-activation versions of Upgrade, Build, or Move — you execute just one activation without any bonus activations from techs. The Action Phase ends immediately once all players have their Summary Tiles flipped.
walkthrough.image_caption: Passing and taking Reactions

walkthrough.title: Colony Ships and Population
walkthrough.text: At any time during your Action or Reaction, you may flip face-up Colony Ships face-down to move Population Cubes from your Control Board to empty Population Squares in sectors you Control. Each cube must come from the Population Track matching the square's color. Gray squares can receive a cube from any track. Advanced Population Squares — those with a star — require the corresponding Advanced Tech to colonize. Population Cubes on sectors produce resources during Upkeep: the highest empty value on each track determines your production.
walkthrough.image_caption: Using Colony Ships to colonize Population Squares

walkthrough.title: Combat Phase: Determining Battles
walkthrough.text: After all players pass, the Combat Phase begins. Identify all sectors containing ships from opposing players or non-player forces. Battles are resolved in descending sector number order. In sectors with more than two opponents, battles are resolved two players at a time in reverse order of entry, with non-player ships battling last. The player who entered first is the Defender; the most recent arrival is the Attacker. If one player Controls the sector, they are always the Defender.
walkthrough.image_caption: Determining battle order and roles

walkthrough.title: Combat Phase: Engagement Rounds
walkthrough.text: Each battle begins with Missiles firing once by ship type in Initiative order. Then Engagement Rounds repeat until one side is eliminated or retreats. Initiative equals the total Initiative symbols on a ship's blueprint. Ties go to the Defender. On each activation, the owning player chooses to Attack or Retreat. To attack, roll one die per Die Symbol on the blueprint — yellow for Ion Cannons, orange for Plasma Cannons and Plasma Missiles, blue for Antimatter Cannons, red for Soliton Cannons. A die result showing bursts is always a Hit; blank is always a Miss. For other results, add your Computer value and subtract the target's Shield value — if the total is 6 or more, it Hits. Damage equals the bursts on the weapon's die symbol. Ships with damage exceeding their Hull value are destroyed.
walkthrough.image_caption: Rolling dice in combat Engagement Rounds

walkthrough.title: Combat Phase: Retreat and Aftermath
walkthrough.text: To Retreat, move all your ships of that type to the edge of a neighboring sector with a Wormhole Connection that you Control and no opponent ships occupy. On their next activation, retreating ships complete the move. If all your remaining ships attempt retreat, you lose the participation Reputation Tile. After all battles in a sector, players draw Reputation Tiles: 1 for participation, plus 1 per destroyed Interceptor/Starbase/Ancient, 2 per Cruiser/Guardian, 3 per Dreadnought, and 3 for the GCDS — maximum 5 draws total. Keep one tile, return the rest. Then attack opponent population, influence uncontrolled sectors, claim Discovery Tiles, and repair all damage.
walkthrough.image_caption: Post-battle resolution steps

walkthrough.title: Diplomatic Relations
walkthrough.text: In games with 4 or more players, during your Action you may propose Diplomatic Relations with another player if you share a Wormhole Connection between controlled sectors, neither player's ships are in the other's sectors, neither holds the Traitor Tile, and both have empty Reputation Track space. If both agree, exchange Ambassador Tiles — each containing a Population Cube from any track — placed on each other's Reputation Tracks. Diplomatic Relations grant VP and increased production. Having your ships in a sector where the other player has ships or control is an Act of Aggression, breaking the relation and earning you the Traitor Tile — a negative 2 VP penalty.
walkthrough.image_caption: Exchanging Ambassador Tiles

walkthrough.title: Upkeep Phase
walkthrough.text: First, all players may use remaining Colony Ships to populate sectors — but not sectors containing opponent ships. Then pay Civilization Upkeep Cost: compare your Money Income (highest exposed value on Money Population Track) to your Upkeep Cost (highest exposed value on Influence Track). Adjust your Money Storage by the difference — positive means gain, negative means pay. If you cannot pay, you must Trade other resources for Money or Abandon sectors until solvent. Abandoning returns the Influence Disc and all Population Cubes. After upkeep, collect Materials and Science production equal to the highest exposed values on those tracks.
walkthrough.image_caption: Paying Upkeep and Producing Resources

walkthrough.title: Cleanup Phase
walkthrough.text: Draw new Tech Tiles from the bag until the correct number for your player count is in the tray: 5 for 2 players, 6 for 3, 7 for 4, 8 for 5, or 9 for 6. Move all Influence Discs from your Action Track back to your Influence Track. Return Population Cubes from your Graveyard to their Population Tracks. Flip all Colony Ships face-up and flip your Summary Tile to the Action Phase Overview side. Move the Round Marker forward one step. A new round begins.
walkthrough.image_caption: Resetting for the next round

walkthrough.title: Game End and Scoring
walkthrough.text: The game ends after the 8th round. Victory Points come from seven sources: Reputation Tiles worth 1-4 VP each, Ambassador Tiles worth 1 VP each, Controlled Sectors worth 1-4 VP each based on the printed value, Monoliths on controlled sectors worth 3 VP each, Discovery Tiles kept VP-side-up worth 2 VP each, progress on Tech Tracks giving VP for filling slots, and Species-specific bonuses. The Traitor Tile costs negative 2 VP. The player with the most VP wins. Ties are broken by total resources in storage.

summary.title: Round Structure
bullets: 8 rounds total, each with 4 phases
Action Phase -> Combat Phase -> Upkeep Phase -> Cleanup Phase
Players take turns clockwise from Start Player during Action Phase

summary.title: Taking Actions and the Influence Cost
bullets: Each action = move leftmost Influence Disc from track to action space
More actions + more controlled sectors = higher Money upkeep cost
Same action can be taken multiple times (stack discs)

summary.title: Action: Explore
bullets: Choose unexplored zone adjacent to your controlled/occupied sector
Flip sector from matching ring stack, place or discard
Wormhole Connection required to place; collect Discovery Tiles from undefended sectors
May place Influence Disc to take Control; use Colony Ships to populate

summary.title: Action: Research
bullets: Pay Science cost for a Tech Tile from the Tech Tray
Place on leftmost space of its category track (Military/Grid/Nano)
Each same-category tech gives a discount on future techs
Unlocks Ship Parts, Structures, or special abilities

summary.title: Action: Upgrade
bullets: Return any Ship Parts from blueprints, then place 1 new part per activation
Must have researched the required tech; energy consumption cannot exceed production
Drives cannot go on Starbases; at least 1 Drive required on mobile ships
All ships of that type upgrade instantly everywhere

summary.title: Action: Build
bullets: Pay Materials: Interceptor 3, Cruiser 5, Dreadnought 8, Starbase 3, Orbital 4, Monolith 10
Place in any Controlled sector; Starbases/Orbitals/Monoliths require techs
Max 1 Monolith + 1 Orbital per sector

summary.title: Action: Move
bullets: Move 1 ship up to its Movement Value (total Drives) in sectors
Must follow Wormhole Connections; cannot enter unexplored zones
Each opponent ship pins 1 of your ships; GCDS pins all ships
Moving into opponent sectors triggers combat and may break diplomacy

summary.title: Action: Influence
bullets: Move Influence Disc to uncontrolled sector (with Wormhole Connection, no opponent ships)
Or move disc back to Influence Track (reducing upkeep)
Removing disc from sector returns all your Population Cubes
Also flips up to 2 used Colony Ships face-up

summary.title: Passing and Reactions
bullets: First to Pass: gain 2 Money + Start Player Tile for next round
After Passing: may take 1 Reaction per turn (single Upgrade, Build, or Move activation)
No tech bonuses on Reactions
Action Phase ends when all players have Passed

summary.title: Colony Ships and Population
bullets: Flip Colony Ships face-down to move Population Cubes to sectors
Cubes must match Population Square color; gray = any track
Advanced squares (star symbol) need corresponding Advanced Tech
Population determines your resource production each round

summary.title: Combat Phase: Determining Battles
bullets: Battles in sectors with opposing ships, resolved in descending sector number
Multi-opponent: resolved 2 at a time in reverse entry order
Sector controller is always Defender; Ancients/Guardians/GCDS are always Defenders

summary.title: Combat Phase: Engagement Rounds
bullets: Missiles fire once first, then repeated Engagement Rounds by Initiative
Roll dice per Die Symbols: bursts = auto-Hit, blank = auto-Miss, else die + Computers - Shields >= 6 = Hit
Damage = burst count on weapon; exceeding Hull Value destroys the ship
Dice colors: yellow (Ion), orange (Plasma), blue (Antimatter), red (Soliton)

summary.title: Combat Phase: Retreat and Aftermath
bullets: Retreat to neighboring Controlled sector with no opponent ships
Reputation Tiles: 1 per participation + 1-3 per destroyed ship type; max 5 draws
Keep 1 tile, return rest to bag
Then: Attack Population, Influence Sectors, claim Discovery Tiles, repair damage

summary.title: Diplomatic Relations
bullets: 4+ players only; requires Wormhole Connection between controlled sectors
Exchange Ambassador Tiles with Population Cubes for VP and production
Act of Aggression breaks relations; aggressor gets Traitor Tile (-2 VP)
Cannot form if either player holds Traitor Tile

summary.title: Upkeep Phase
bullets: Use Colony Ships (not into opponent-occupied sectors)
Pay Upkeep: Money Income minus Civilization Upkeep Cost
Bankruptcy: Trade resources or Abandon sectors to pay
Produce Materials and Science from highest exposed track values

summary.title: Cleanup Phase
bullets: Draw new Tech Tiles for tray (2p=5, 3p=6, 4p=7, 5p=8, 6p=9)
Return Influence Discs from Action Track to Influence Track
Return Graveyard cubes to Population Tracks; flip Colony Ships and Summary Tile
Advance Round Marker; begin new round

summary.title: Game End and Scoring
bullets: Game ends after Round 8
VP: Reputation Tiles (1-4) + Ambassadors (1) + Sectors (1-4) + Monoliths (3) + Discovery Tiles (2) + Tech Track progress + Species bonuses
Traitor Tile = -2 VP; ties broken by total resources in storage

walkthrough.title: Welcome to the Galaxy
walkthrough.text: In this practice tutorial, we will walk through a scripted first round with four example players: Player 1 (Terran), Player 2 (Planta), Player 3 (Mechanema), and Player 4 (Hydran Progress). Each will take different actions so you can see the core mechanics. Follow along even if you have a different player count or species. Ready? The galaxy awaits.
walkthrough.image_caption: Four civilizations ready to begin

walkthrough.title: Round 1 Begins
walkthrough.text: Everyone has their Starting Sectors placed, Influence Discs on the track, Population Cubes producing resources, and an Interceptor ready to go. Player 1 holds the Start Player Tile, so they act first. Remember — every action you take costs an Influence Disc from your track, which increases your upkeep cost. Early on you have plenty of discs, but planning ahead is essential.

walkthrough.title: Player 1's Turn: Explore
walkthrough.text: Player 1 decides to Explore. They move their leftmost Influence Disc from their Influence Track to the Explore Action Space. Their species gets 2 Explore Activations. For the first, they choose an empty zone adjacent to their Starting Sector and flip a tile from the Middle Sector stack. It has a Discovery Tile symbol and a Money Population Square. They orient it with a Wormhole Connection to their Starting Sector, place a face-down Discovery Tile on it, and take Control by moving another Influence Disc from their track to the new sector's Influence Space. They flip a Colony Ship face-down to colonize the Money square. For their second activation, they flip an Outer Sector but discard it — the placement did not line up well.
walkthrough.image_caption: Exploring and placing a new sector

walkthrough.title: Why Exploring First Is Smart
walkthrough.text: Exploring early gives you first pick of unclaimed zones and access to Discovery Tiles. Each new sector with population squares increases your resource production. Player 1 just added Money production, which will help pay for the upkeep cost of all those Influence Discs they are spending. Getting sectors early also expands your Wormhole network for future turns.

walkthrough.title: Player 2's Turn: Explore (Planta's Advantage)
walkthrough.text: Player 2 is Planta — they get 2 Explore Activations per action and 4 Colony Ships. They explore aggressively, placing one Middle and one Outer Sector, colonizing Population Squares immediately with Colony Ships. Planta's extra VP per controlled sector makes early expansion especially valuable. They take the Discovery Tile from the undefended sector, reveal it — it is worth 2 VP — and keep it face-up next to their Species Board.
walkthrough.image_caption: Planta expands rapidly with multiple explorations

walkthrough.title: Player 3's Turn: Research
walkthrough.text: Player 3 is Mechanema — they start with the Positron Computer tech and have cheaper build costs. They decide to Research, moving an Influence Disc to the Research Action Space. They take the Plasma Cannon tech from the Tech Tray, paying its Science cost. Mechanema gets 2 Research Activations, so they also grab Nanorobots for a second research, netting a discount because both are in the Nano category. Player 3 is setting up their military engine early.
walkthrough.image_caption: Researching Technology from the Tech Tray

walkthrough.title: Player 4's Turn: The Mistake — Overextending
walkthrough.text: Player 4 is Hydran Progress — they start with Advanced Labs and high Science production. Eager to use their research advantage, Player 4 takes Explore and places two new sectors, putting Influence Discs on both. But they already moved one disc for the action itself plus the two for control. That is three Influence Discs off their track in a single turn. Their Civilization Upkeep Cost just jumped significantly. They have not built any Money infrastructure to pay for this expansion.
walkthrough.image_caption: Player 4 overextends with too many Influence Discs

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Watch Your Upkeep Cost
walkthrough.text: This is the most common beginner mistake in Eclipse. Every Influence Disc you remove — whether for actions or sector control — exposes a higher number on your Influence Track, increasing your Civilization Upkeep Cost. If your Money Income cannot cover it, you must Trade resources or Abandon sectors during Upkeep. Player 4 will learn this the hard way in a few turns. Always check the rightmost empty space on your Influence Track before committing to actions.

walkthrough.title: Player 1's Second Turn: Build
walkthrough.text: Player 1 takes the Build Action. They pay 5 Materials to build a Cruiser in their Starting Sector and 3 Materials to build an Interceptor in their newly explored sector. Two build activations, 8 Materials spent. They now have ships in two sectors — a Cruiser for power and an Interceptor for scouting. Building early means those ships are available for combat defense and future movement.
walkthrough.image_caption: Building a Cruiser and Interceptor

walkthrough.title: Player 2's Second Turn: Build
walkthrough.text: Player 2 builds a Starbase in their Starting Sector. Planta starts with the Starbase tech, so they can build one immediately for 3 Materials. Starbases are immobile but participate in combat — they defend your sector without needing to move. A smart defensive play for a civilization focused on expansion.

walkthrough.title: Player 3's Second Turn: Upgrade
walkthrough.text: Player 3 takes the Upgrade Action. Mechanema gets 3 Upgrade Activations per action — a major advantage. They remove the default Electron Computer from their Interceptor blueprint and place a Positron Computer, then add a Plasma Cannon to the Cruiser blueprint and an Ion Cannon in the remaining slot. Their ships are now significantly more dangerous. Remember, all Interceptors and Cruisers they own anywhere on the board are instantly upgraded.
walkthrough.image_caption: Upgrading Ship Blueprints

walkthrough.title: Player 4's Mistake: Researching Without Resources
walkthrough.text: Player 4 takes Research again — they want more tech. They spend their remaining Science on two expensive techs. But now their Science Storage is nearly empty, and they still have not colonized the Population Squares in their new sectors because they ran out of Colony Ships. Their sectors are producing almost nothing. Meanwhile, Player 3 has been building an efficient war machine and Player 1 is generating resources every round.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Balance Expansion with Economy
walkthrough.text: Researching powerful techs is exciting, but techs without resources to use them are wasted potential. You need Population Cubes on sectors to produce Materials, Science, and Money. Player 4 should have explored, colonized, and built up production before investing in expensive research. The golden rule: expand your economy to fund your strategy, not the other way around.

walkthrough.title: Player 1 Passes
walkthrough.text: Player 1 decides they have done enough for this round and Passes. They flip their Summary Tile to the Reactions Overview side. Since they are the first to Pass, they receive 2 Money and the Start Player Tile for next round. Being first to pass is not weakness — it saves Influence Discs and gives you the initiative next round. From now on, Player 1 can only take Reactions — single activations of Upgrade, Build, or Move.

walkthrough.title: Player 4's Mistake: Forgetting Pinning
walkthrough.text: Player 4 takes the Move Action and sends their lone Interceptor into a sector adjacent to Player 3, hoping to scout. But Player 3 already has two Interceptors there. Player 4's single ship is Pinned — it cannot leave because each of Player 3's ships pins one of Player 4's ships. Player 4 is now stuck in a sector they do not control, heading into combat against a player with upgraded weapons. This did not go well.
walkthrough.image_caption: Player 4's Interceptor pinned by Player 3's ships

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Check Pinning Before Moving
walkthrough.text: Before sending ships anywhere, count the opponent ships already there. Each enemy ship pins one of yours. If you move in with fewer ships than the opponent has, your ships are trapped and must fight. Always have numerical superiority or a plan to reinforce before engaging. Player 4 should have built more ships before moving aggressively.

walkthrough.title: Remaining Players Pass
walkthrough.text: Players 2, 3, and 4 each pass over subsequent turns. Player 3 takes a Build Reaction first, adding another Interceptor in their contested sector for the upcoming battle. Once all Summary Tiles are flipped, the Action Phase ends and we move to the Combat Phase.

walkthrough.title: Combat Phase Preview
walkthrough.text: Player 4's lone Interceptor faces Player 3's three Interceptors. Initiative is calculated from each ship's blueprint. Player 3's upgraded weapons make this fight very one-sided. Missiles fire first, then Engagement Rounds begin. Player 4's Interceptor is quickly destroyed. Player 3 draws Reputation Tiles — 1 for participation plus 1 for the destroyed Interceptor. They keep the higher-value tile. Player 4 gets nothing because they did not destroy any ships.

walkthrough.title: Upkeep Phase
walkthrough.text: Now everyone resolves Upkeep simultaneously. Compare Money Income to Civilization Upkeep Cost. Player 1 and Player 2 are in good shape — their early colonization pays off. Player 3 spent on military but has decent economy. Player 4, however, discovers their upkeep cost exceeds their income. They have to Trade Science for Money to stay solvent. This is the consequence of overexpansion without economic backing.

walkthrough.title: Cleanup and Round 2 Preview
walkthrough.text: Return Influence Discs from Action Tracks. Draw new Tech Tiles for the tray. Flip Colony Ships face-up. Advance the Round Marker. Seven more rounds to go. Player 1 has position and economy. Player 2 has territory. Player 3 has military power. Player 4 has learned three hard lessons but has powerful techs that will pay off if they stabilize their economy.

walkthrough.title: Congratulations
walkthrough.text: You have seen all six actions in practice — Explore, Research, Upgrade, Build, Move, and Influence — plus Passing and Reactions. You watched Player 4 learn from three common mistakes: overextending with Influence Discs, researching without economic support, and moving into combat without checking pinning. You have seen combat resolution, upkeep payment, and resource production. You are ready to lead your civilization.

walkthrough.title: Before You Launch
walkthrough.text:
Before you dive into a full game, here are three things that can help.

First — check out General Tips for strategy basics and the most common pitfalls.

Second — reference the Appendix for every technology, ship part, species, and game term.

And most importantly — ask your personal GM Guide for help anytime. Just tap the Q&A tab and ask any question during your game. May your civilization eclipse all others.

summary.title: Welcome to the Galaxy
bullets: Scripted tutorial with 4 players: Terran, Planta, Mechanema, Hydran Progress
Covers a complete first round including all six action types
Follow along even with different player count or species

summary.title: Round 1 Begins
bullets: Starting Sectors placed, Influence Discs on track, one Interceptor each
Every action costs an Influence Disc — plan ahead
Player 1 (Start Player) acts first

summary.title: Player 1's Turn: Explore
bullets: Move Influence Disc to Explore Action Space
Flip sector tile, orient with Wormhole Connection, place Discovery Tile
Take Control with another Influence Disc; colonize with Colony Ship

summary.title: Why Exploring First Is Smart
bullets: First pick of unclaimed zones and Discovery Tiles
New sectors with population = more resource production
Expands your Wormhole network for future actions

summary.title: Player 2's Turn: Explore (Planta's Advantage)
bullets: Planta gets 2 Explore Activations + 4 Colony Ships
Places two sectors and colonizes immediately
Discovery Tile kept as 2 VP

summary.title: Player 3's Turn: Research
bullets: Mechanema researches Plasma Cannon and Nanorobots
Same-category discount applies to second tech
Setting up military engine early

summary.title: Player 4's Turn: The Mistake — Overextending
bullets: Places 2 sectors + action = 3 Influence Discs spent in one turn
Civilization Upkeep Cost jumps dramatically
No Money infrastructure to support the expansion

summary.title: Lesson: Watch Your Upkeep Cost
bullets: Every Influence Disc off your track increases Upkeep Cost
Check rightmost empty space before committing to actions
If income cannot cover upkeep, you must Trade or Abandon sectors

summary.title: Player 1's Second Turn: Build
bullets: Builds Cruiser (5 Materials) + Interceptor (3 Materials)
Ships in two sectors for defense and scouting
Building early = more combat options

summary.title: Player 2's Second Turn: Build
bullets: Planta builds a Starbase (3 Materials) for home defense
Starbases are immobile but fight in combat
Smart defensive play for an expansion-focused species

summary.title: Player 3's Second Turn: Upgrade
bullets: Mechanema gets 3 Upgrade Activations per action
Replaces Electron Computer with Positron Computer, adds weapons
All ships of each type upgrade instantly everywhere

summary.title: Player 4's Mistake: Researching Without Resources
bullets: Spends all Science on expensive techs
No Colony Ships left to populate new sectors
Sectors producing nothing — tech without economy is wasted

summary.title: Lesson: Balance Expansion with Economy
bullets: Techs without resources to use them are wasted
Colonize Population Squares to fund your strategy
Expand economy first, then invest in expensive techs

summary.title: Player 1 Passes
bullets: First to Pass = 2 Money + Start Player Tile next round
Summary Tile flips to Reactions Overview
Can still take single-activation Reactions (Upgrade/Build/Move)

summary.title: Player 4's Mistake: Forgetting Pinning
bullets: Moves lone Interceptor into sector with 2 enemy ships
Each enemy ship pins 1 of yours — Interceptor is trapped
Heading into combat outnumbered and outgunned

summary.title: Lesson: Check Pinning Before Moving
bullets: Count opponent ships before moving in
Fewer ships than the enemy = you are trapped
Build up forces before engaging in combat

summary.title: Remaining Players Pass
bullets: Player 3 takes a Build Reaction to add a ship first
Once all Summary Tiles are flipped, Action Phase ends
Combat Phase begins

summary.title: Combat Phase Preview
bullets: Player 4's Interceptor vs Player 3's three Interceptors
Missiles fire first, then Engagement Rounds by Initiative
Player 3 draws Reputation Tiles; Player 4 draws nothing

summary.title: Upkeep Phase
bullets: Compare Money Income to Civilization Upkeep Cost
Players 1-3 are solvent; Player 4 must Trade to pay upkeep
Consequence of overexpansion without economic backing

summary.title: Cleanup and Round 2 Preview
bullets: Return Influence Discs, draw new Techs, flip Colony Ships
7 rounds remain; each player has distinct strengths
Player 4 has powerful techs but needs to stabilize economy

summary.title: Congratulations
bullets: You have practiced all 6 actions plus Passing and Reactions
Player 4 learned 3 mistakes: overextending, no economy, ignoring pinning
You have seen combat, upkeep, and production in action

summary.title: Before You Launch
bullets: Check out General Tips for strategy basics and common pitfalls
Reference the Appendix for every tech, ship part, species, and term
Ask your personal GM Guide — tap Q&A for instant answers during play

walkthrough.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
walkthrough.text: Before diving into strategy, here are the biggest beginner traps. First, overextending with Influence Discs — taking too many actions and controlling too many sectors without Money to pay upkeep. Second, ignoring the economy and rushing expensive techs or ships without Population Cubes producing resources. Third, moving ships into combat without checking pinning rules or the enemy's upgraded weapons. Fourth, forgetting that Colony Ships are the only way to move Population Cubes to sectors — no Colony Ships means no production growth. Keep these in mind and you will avoid the most painful first-game moments.

walkthrough.title: Early Exploration is Critical
walkthrough.text: The first two rounds should focus heavily on Explore. New sectors mean new Population Squares, which mean more resource production every round for the rest of the game. Grabbing sectors early also gives you better Wormhole network options and first access to Discovery Tiles. Aim to control at least three to four sectors by the end of round two. Every round you delay exploring is a round of production you will never get back.

walkthrough.title: Manage Your Influence Economy
walkthrough.text: Think of your Influence Track as a budget. Every disc you remove increases your costs. Before taking an action, glance at your Influence Track and check what your upkeep will be at the end of the round. A good rule of thumb: never let your Civilization Upkeep Cost exceed your Money Income by more than what you have in storage. If you are going to be short, plan to Pass early and claim that 2 Money bonus. Trading resources for Money is expensive — it is much better to prevent the shortage in the first place.

walkthrough.title: Colonize Aggressively with Colony Ships
walkthrough.text: Colony Ships are your lifeline. Every Population Cube you place on a sector increases your production. Use Colony Ships immediately when you explore a new sector — do not wait. If you run out of Colony Ships, take the Influence Action to flip them back face-up. Prioritize Money Population Squares first, since Money pays your upkeep. Then Science for research, then Materials for building. Gray Population Squares are flexible — place cubes from whichever track you need most.

walkthrough.title: Research with Purpose
walkthrough.text: Do not research random techs — have a plan. If you want military dominance, target weapons and computers along the Military track for discounts. If you want to build an economic empire, focus on Nano techs like Orbital, Monolith, and the Advanced colonization techs. Buy techs in ascending cost order within a category to maximize discounts. Remember that each filled tech track slot gives you VP at game end, so deep investment in one or two categories often beats scattered purchases across all three.

walkthrough.title: Upgrade Before You Fight
walkthrough.text: Default ship blueprints are weak. An unupgraded Interceptor with an Ion Cannon and Nuclear Drive is no match for a ship with Plasma Cannons, better Computers, and Shields. Before engaging in combat, spend at least one turn Upgrading. Focus on three things: weapons that deal more damage, Computers that help you hit, and enough Drives to maintain mobility. Shields are situational — they only matter if the opponent has weapons that can be offset. Energy management is the key constraint: every weapon and drive consumes energy, so plan your energy sources first.

walkthrough.title: Timing Your Pass
walkthrough.text: Passing is not just giving up — it is a strategic decision. Passing first gives you 2 Money and the Start Player Tile, meaning you get first pick of actions next round. If you have already done your key actions and your Influence Track is getting dangerously low, Pass early. After Passing, you can still take Reactions — single Build, Upgrade, or Move activations — which is often enough to respond to threats. Many experienced players deliberately pass after four or five actions, saving discs for a stronger position in the next round.

walkthrough.title: Watch the Scoreboard
walkthrough.text: Eclipse has seven scoring categories, and you should be working toward at least three or four of them. Controlled Sectors are the most reliable source — each is worth 1 to 4 VP. Reputation Tiles from combat can be very lucrative. Monoliths are a steady 3 VP each. Discovery Tiles give 2 VP when kept face-up. Tech Track progress rewards deep research. Ambassador Tiles are free VP with minimal risk. The Traitor Tile is negative 2 VP — only break diplomatic relations if the territory you gain is worth more. In the final rounds, calculate which VP sources are most efficient for your remaining actions.

summary.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
bullets: Overextending Influence Discs — upkeep cost spirals out of control
Ignoring economy — expensive techs without production to use them
Moving into combat without checking pinning or enemy upgrades
Forgetting Colony Ships are the only way to grow production

summary.title: Early Exploration is Critical
bullets: Explore heavily in rounds 1-2 to grab Population Squares
Each round without new sectors = lost production forever
Aim for 3-4 controlled sectors by end of round 2
Early sectors = better Wormhole network + Discovery Tiles

summary.title: Manage Your Influence Economy
bullets: Every Influence Disc removed increases your Upkeep Cost
Never let Upkeep exceed Income by more than your Money storage
Pass early for 2 Money + Start Player if running low
Trading resources for Money is expensive — prevent the shortage

summary.title: Colonize Aggressively with Colony Ships
bullets: Use Colony Ships immediately when exploring new sectors
Prioritize: Money squares first, then Science, then Materials
Gray squares = flexible, place from whichever track you need
Influence Action flips Colony Ships face-up for reuse

summary.title: Research with Purpose
bullets: Buy techs in ascending cost order for maximum discount
Deep investment in 1-2 categories beats scattered purchases
Military track for weapons; Nano track for economy/structures
Filled tech track slots = VP at game end

summary.title: Upgrade Before You Fight
bullets: Default blueprints are very weak — always Upgrade before combat
Key upgrades: weapons (damage), Computers (accuracy), Drives (mobility)
Energy management is the main constraint — plan energy sources first
Shields matter only if opponent uses mid-level weapons

summary.title: Timing Your Pass
bullets: First to Pass = 2 Money + Start Player next round
Reactions after Passing still let you Build, Upgrade, or Move once
Deliberately pass after 4-5 actions to save discs for next round
Passing is strategic positioning, not giving up

summary.title: Watch the Scoreboard
bullets: 7 scoring categories — work toward at least 3-4
Controlled Sectors (1-4 VP) are the most reliable source
Monoliths (3 VP), Discovery Tiles (2 VP), Reputation Tiles (1-4 VP)
Traitor Tile (-2 VP) — only break diplomacy if the gain outweighs the penalty

walkthrough.title: Economic Engine Strategy
walkthrough.text: The economic approach focuses on maximizing resource production and converting it into VP through Monoliths, Orbitals, and sector control. Rush the Nano tech track: Orbital lets you add a Population Square to any sector, Advanced Labs and Advanced Mining open star-marked squares, and Monolith gives you a 3 VP structure. Build Orbitals in your highest-value sectors and populate them with Science or Money cubes. By mid-game, you should be producing 20-plus of each resource per round, letting you outbuild and outresearch everyone. The key is to avoid combat — you do not need to fight if your empire is so productive that you can build faster than opponents can destroy.

walkthrough.title: Military Dominance Strategy
walkthrough.text: The military approach aims to conquer sectors and farm Reputation Tiles for massive VP. Invest early in the Military tech track: Plasma Cannon, Phase Shield, and Tachyon Source are your priority. Upgrade your Cruisers with high-damage weapons and good Computers, then build a fleet of three to four ships before attacking. Target sectors with high VP values and weak defenders. Each battle draws Reputation Tiles — a single Dreadnought kill is worth 3 draws, and you keep the best tile. Over the course of the game, five to six combat tiles worth 2-4 VP each can put you ahead of any peaceful builder.

walkthrough.title: Missile Alpha Strike
walkthrough.text: Missiles fire once before regular Engagement Rounds, and high-initiative ships fire their missiles first. The Plasma Missile tech is devastating — each ship part grants two orange dice that fire before normal combat. Upgrade your Interceptors with Plasma Missiles and Fusion Drives for high initiative. A fleet of four Interceptors firing 8 orange dice before the enemy shoots can destroy two or three ships in the opening volley. This strategy wins battles quickly but requires careful energy management — Plasma Missiles consume 2 energy each.

walkthrough.title: Shield Wall Defense
walkthrough.text: Shields subtract from opponent die rolls, making it harder to hit your ships. A ship with Phase Shields and Gauss Shields combined can have a Shield value of 3 or more. Against standard Ion Cannons, this means opponents need natural rolls of 6 (bursts) to hit — a probability of roughly one in six. Pair Shields with Hull upgrades to absorb any damage that gets through. This strategy is particularly strong with Starbases, which cannot move but have extra Ship Part spaces for stacking defensive upgrades. A Starbase with shields and hull in a key chokepoint can hold off entire fleets.

walkthrough.title: Discovery Tile Rushing
walkthrough.text: Discovery Tiles are among the most underrated VP sources. Each tile kept face-up is worth 2 VP, and some front-side rewards — like Ancient Tech, Ancient Cruiser, or Ancient Ship Parts — can be worth even more in practical value. Explore aggressively into sectors with Discovery Tile symbols and destroy Ancients early to claim those tiles. The Descendants of Draco species excels at this — they can coexist with Ancients and score 1 VP per Ancient on the board. By exploring deep and collecting six to eight Discovery Tiles, you can accumulate 12-16 VP from tiles alone.

walkthrough.title: Tech Track VP Racing
walkthrough.text: Each Tech Track scores VP based on how many slots are filled: 1 VP for the first few techs escalating to 5 VP for filling an entire track. Focusing research on a single category — especially Nano, which has the broadest utility — lets you stack discounts and fill the track cheaply. Hydran Progress excels at this with 2 Research Activations per action and high starting Science. Pair this with Rare Techs placed on your chosen track for extra slots. Four or five techs in one category can yield 3-5 VP just from track progress, on top of the abilities those techs grant.

walkthrough.title: Diplomatic VP Farming
walkthrough.text: In games with 4 or more players, Ambassador Tiles are free VP — 1 VP each with no combat risk. They also provide production since the exchanged Population Cube counts toward your income. Form diplomatic relations as early as possible with neighbors who share a Wormhole Connection. Three Ambassador Tiles is 3 VP plus increased production from three cubes. The risk is that breaking relations earns the Traitor Tile. Be selective about who you ally with — choose players whose expansion direction does not conflict with yours. Never break relations unless the territory gained is worth at least 4 VP (enough to offset the 2 VP Traitor penalty plus the lost Ambassador VP).

walkthrough.title: Species-Specific Strategies
walkthrough.text: Each alien species has unique strengths. Planta should explore aggressively — with 2 Explore Activations, 4 Colony Ships, and 1 bonus VP per controlled sector, controlling eight sectors is realistic for 12-plus VP from territory alone. Mechanema should rush upgrades — 3 Upgrade Activations and cheaper builds make military domination efficient. Hydran Progress should deep-research one category — 2 Research Activations and Advanced Labs give an unmatched tech advantage. Eridani Empire starts with Reputation Tiles and strong weapons but fewer Influence Discs, so they must be surgical with actions. Descendants of Draco benefit from leaving Ancients alive for VP — explore around them rather than destroying them. Orion Hegemony starts with a Cruiser and high initiative, perfect for early aggression.

walkthrough.title: Endgame Optimization
walkthrough.text: In rounds 7 and 8, shift from building your engine to maximizing VP. Count your projected score and compare to opponents. If you are behind in Reputation Tiles, pick a fight you can win to draw tiles. If you are behind in sectors, use the Influence Action to claim uncontrolled sectors or attack weakly defended ones. Build a Monolith in your most secure sector for 3 easy VP. If you have Discovery Tiles on their reward side, consider whether keeping one for 2 VP would have been better. Every action in the final rounds should translate directly into VP — no more speculative expansion or unnecessary research.

walkthrough.title: Rare Tech Power Plays
walkthrough.text: Rare Techs are one-of-a-kind and can define a winning strategy. Antimatter Splitter lets you freely distribute Antimatter Cannon damage across targets — devastating in fleet battles. Cloaking Device means opponents need two ships to pin each of yours, making your fleet much more mobile. Improved Logistics gives an extra Move Activation per Move Action, letting you project force across the board. Warp Portal connects your sector to all other Warp Portal sectors for 1 VP and massive strategic mobility. Metasynthesis opens all Advanced Population Squares — combined with Orbitals, this generates enormous production. Grab Rare Techs early before opponents take them — they can go on any tech track, giving both the tech's ability and a discount in your chosen category.

bullets: Rush Nano track: Orbital, Advanced Labs/Mining, Monolith
Build Orbitals for extra Population Squares in high-value sectors
Target 20+ of each resource per round by mid-game
Avoid combat — outproduce opponents instead

summary.title: Military Dominance Strategy
bullets: Invest in Military track: Plasma Cannon, Phase Shield, Tachyon Source
Build 3-4 upgraded ships before attacking
Target high-VP sectors with weak defenders
5-6 Reputation Tiles worth 2-4 VP each = major VP swing

summary.title: Missile Alpha Strike
bullets: Plasma Missiles fire before regular combat — devastating first volley
Upgrade Interceptors with Plasma Missiles + Fusion Drives for high initiative
4 Interceptors = 8 orange dice before enemy fires
High energy cost — plan Energy Sources carefully

summary.title: Shield Wall Defense
bullets: Phase Shields + Gauss Shields = Shield value 3+, opponents need natural 6 to hit
Pair with Hull upgrades to absorb the hits that do land
Starbases are ideal for shield stacking — extra Ship Part spaces
Chokepoint Starbases can hold off entire fleets

summary.title: Discovery Tile Rushing
bullets: Each tile = 2 VP face-up; front-side rewards can be even more valuable
Explore aggressively into Discovery Tile sectors
Descendants of Draco score 1 VP per Ancient alive — explore around them
6-8 tiles = 12-16 VP, a game-winning total

bullets: Filling a track = up to 5 VP from track progress alone
Focus on one category for stacking discounts
Hydran Progress: 2 Research Activations + high Science = ideal
Rare Techs can go on any track for extra discount + slot

Form relations early with non-threatening neighbors
3 Ambassadors = 3 VP + income boost
Only break relations if gain exceeds 4 VP (2 VP penalty + lost Ambassador)

summary.title: Species-Specific Strategies
bullets: Planta: explore/colonize 8 sectors = 12+ VP from territory
Mechanema: 3 Upgrade Activations + cheap builds = military machine
Hydran Progress: deep research one category for tech VP + abilities
Orion Hegemony: Cruiser start + high initiative = early aggression

summary.title: Endgame Optimization
bullets: Rounds 7-8: every action must translate directly to VP
Behind in Reputation? Pick a winnable fight
Behind in sectors? Influence or attack weak sectors
Build a Monolith (3 VP) in your most secure sector

summary.title: Rare Tech Power Plays
bullets: Antimatter Splitter: split Antimatter Cannon damage across targets
Cloaking Device: opponents need 2 ships to pin each of yours
Improved Logistics: +1 Move Activation per action
Metasynthesis: opens all Advanced squares — massive production boost

entries.definition: Discover new Sector tiles by flipping them from the ring stacks and placing them adjacent to your controlled or occupied sectors. Requires a Wormhole Connection to place. Lets you take Control and claim Discovery Tiles from undefended sectors.

Acquire Tech Tiles from the Tech Tray by paying their Science cost. Techs are placed on your Species Board's category tracks and unlock Ship Parts, Structures, and special abilities. Same-category techs provide cumulative discounts.

Modify your Ship Blueprints by returning and placing Ship Part Tiles. All ships of that type are instantly upgraded. Requires the corresponding tech for most parts. Energy Consumption must not exceed Energy Production.

Construct Ships or Structures in sectors you Control by paying Materials cost. Ship types: Interceptor (3), Cruiser (5), Dreadnought (8), Starbase (3). Structures: Orbital (4), Monolith (10). Some require researched techs.
Reposition Ships up to their Movement Value in sectors, following Wormhole Connections. Pinned ships cannot leave their sector. Cannot move to unexplored zones.

Move Influence Discs between your Influence Track and sectors. Can take Control of uncontrolled sectors with a Wormhole Connection, or return discs to reduce upkeep. Also flips up to 2 Colony Ships face-up.
End your actions for the round. First to Pass gains 2 Money and the Start Player Tile. After Passing, you may take Reactions — weaker single-activation versions of Upgrade, Build, or Move.
A weaker version of Upgrade, Build, or Move taken after Passing. Grants only 1 activation without tech bonuses. Costs an Influence Disc placed on the Reaction Track of your Summary Tile.

The smallest and cheapest ship type. Costs 3 Materials to build. Each player has 8. Fast and expendable, useful for scouting, pinning, and missile rushes. Default blueprint: Ion Cannon, Nuclear Drive, Nuclear Source.
A medium ship costing 5 Materials. Each player has 4. Larger blueprint grid allows more Ship Parts than Interceptors. Good balance of firepower and durability.
The largest combat ship, costing 8 Materials. Each player has 2. Biggest blueprint grid with the most Ship Part spaces. Destroys them earn opponents 3 Reputation Tile draws.
An immobile defensive ship costing 3 Materials. Requires the Starbase tech. Each player has 4. Cannot have Drive Ship Parts. Defends sectors and has a large Ship Part grid for stacking upgrades.
Each opponent ship in a sector pins one of your ships, preventing it from moving. The GCDS pins all ships in the Galactic Center. Cloaking Device requires 2 ships to pin each of your ships.
The grid on your Species Board showing each ship type's Ship Parts and abilities. All ships of a type share the same blueprint. Pre-printed parts cannot be removed. Modified with the Upgrade Action.
The total value of Drive symbols on a Ship Blueprint. Determines how many sectors a ship can move in one Move Activation.
Default weapon on most starting ship blueprints. Rolls 1 yellow die per symbol. Low damage (1 burst) but no tech required. Hits on 6+.
An upgrade weapon requiring the Plasma Cannon tech. Rolls 1 orange die per symbol. Deals 2 burst damage per hit. Consumes 2 energy.

A powerful weapon requiring the Antimatter Cannon tech. Rolls 1 blue die per symbol. Deals 4 burst damage per hit. Consumes 4 energy. With the Antimatter Splitter rare tech, damage can be split across targets.
A Rare Tech weapon. Rolls 1 red die per symbol. Extremely high damage. Rare and powerful, only available if the Soliton Cannon Rare Tech is drawn.
A missile weapon requiring the Plasma Missile tech. Fires once before Engagement Rounds in Initiative order. Rolls 2 orange dice per symbol. Devastating in alpha-strike builds.
A Rare Tech missile weapon. Fires before Engagement Rounds. More powerful than Plasma Missiles. Only available if the Flux Missile Rare Tech is drawn.
Default computer on most starting blueprints. Adds +1 to die rolls for determining hits. No tech required. The baseline for accuracy.
An upgraded computer requiring the Positron Computer tech. Adds +2 to die rolls. Better accuracy but higher energy cost.
An advanced computer requiring the Gluon Computer tech. Adds +3 to die rolls. High accuracy at high energy cost.
A shield requiring the Gauss Shield tech. Subtracts 1 from opponent die rolls targeting your ship. Reduces enemy accuracy.
An upgraded shield requiring the Phase Shield tech. Subtracts 2 from opponent die rolls. Strong defense against mid-tier weapons.
A Rare Tech shield. High shield value for maximum damage reduction. Only available if the Absorption Shield Rare Tech is drawn.
A Rare Tech ship part providing a unique defensive ability. Only available if the Conifold Field Rare Tech is drawn.
Determines how much damage a ship can absorb before being destroyed. A ship is destroyed when damage exceeds its total Hull value. Default hull starts at 0 for Interceptors.
An upgraded hull part requiring the Improved Hull tech. Adds hull points to absorb more damage before destruction.
A Rare Tech hull part. Provides additional hull points. Only available if the Sentient Hull Rare Tech is drawn.
Default drive on most starting ship blueprints. Provides 1 Movement Value and 1 Initiative. No tech required.
An upgraded drive requiring the Fusion Drive tech. Provides 2 Movement Value and 2 Initiative. Consumes 2 energy.
An advanced drive requiring the Tachyon Drive tech. Provides 3 Movement Value and 3 Initiative. High energy consumption.
A Rare Tech drive. Provides high mobility. Only available if the Transition Drive Rare Tech is drawn.
Default energy source on most starting ship blueprints. Provides 3 Energy Production. No tech required.
An upgraded energy source requiring the Fusion Source tech. Provides 6 Energy Production. Enables more powerful ship parts.
An advanced energy source requiring the Tachyon Source tech. Provides 9 Energy Production. Powers the most demanding blueprints.
A Rare Tech energy source. Provides the highest Energy Production in the game. Only available if the Zero-Point Source Rare Tech is drawn.
A special Ancient Ship Part energy source found via Discovery Tiles. Placed outside the Ship Blueprint grid. Provides additional Energy Production.
Determines the order of ship activation in combat. Calculated by totaling Initiative symbols on a ship's blueprint (mainly from Drives). Higher initiative fires first. Ties favor the Defender.
A round of combat where each ship type activates in Initiative order. The owning player chooses to Attack or Retreat. Repeated until one side is destroyed or retreats.
A die result that deals damage. Burst symbols are automatic hits. Other results: die value + Computers - target Shields >= 6 means a Hit. Blank faces are automatic misses.
Measured in burst symbols on a weapon's Die Symbol. Each burst causes one point of damage. Damage exceeding a ship's Hull Value destroys it. Excess damage does not carry over to other ships.

Moving all ships of one type toward a neighboring Controlled sector with a Wormhole Connection and no opponent ships. Takes two activations to complete. If all your ships retreat, you lose the participation Reputation Tile.

After all battles resolve, remaining ships attack opponent Population Cubes in their sector. Each ship attacks once with non-missile weapons. Population Cubes have 0 Shield Value. Destroyed cubes go to the Graveyard.
When neither player can destroy the other's ships (e.g., both unarmed or only have missiles). The Attacker must Retreat or their ships are destroyed.
Drawn after combat and placed face-down on your Reputation Track. Worth 1-4 VP. Draw 1 for participation + 1-3 per destroyed ship type. Maximum 5 draws per battle. Keep 1, return the rest.
Exchanged when forming Diplomatic Relations (4+ player games). Worth 1 VP each. Contains a Population Cube that adds to your production. Placed on your Reputation Track.
Given to the player who commits an Act of Aggression breaking Diplomatic Relations. Worth -2 VP. Prevents forming new diplomatic relations while held. Passed to the next traitor.

Found on explored sectors and sectors defended by Ancients. Double-sided: front shows a reward (resources, tech, ship parts), back shows 2 VP. Must choose which side when claiming. Undefended tiles claimed during Explore; defended tiles claimed after destroying Ancients.

Flipped face-down to move one Population Cube from your Control Board to an empty Population Square in a controlled sector. The only way to place Population Cubes on sectors. Flipped face-up by the Influence Action and during Cleanup.

Marks sectors you Control and tracks actions taken. Moving a disc from the Influence Track to an action space or sector increases your Civilization Upkeep Cost. You have 13 on the track plus 3 extras for specific techs.

Placed on sector Population Squares to produce resources. Removed from the three Population Tracks on your Control Board. The highest exposed value on each track determines your production. Destroyed cubes go to Graveyards and return during Cleanup.

Tracks your current resource stockpile on the Storage Track. Orange for Materials, pink for Science, brown for Money. Moves forward when gaining resources, backward when paying. Can exceed 40 by flipping the marker.
Resource used for Building Ships and Structures. Produced by Population Cubes on orange Population Squares. Tracked by the orange Storage Marker.
Resource used for Researching Techs. Produced by Population Cubes on white/Science Population Squares. Tracked by the pink Storage Marker.
Resource used for paying Civilization Upkeep Cost. Produced by Population Cubes on pink/Money Population Squares. Also gained by being first to Pass (2 Money). Can be Traded for other resources.

At any time, pay your Species Board's Trade Value in one resource to gain 1 of any other resource. Most species have a Trade Value of 3 (pay 3 of one to gain 1 of another). Can be done any number of times.

A link between two adjacent sectors where both edges contain a Wormhole symbol. Required for Moving, Exploring, and Influencing between sectors. The Wormhole Generator tech allows connections with only one Wormhole.
A hexagonal tile representing a star system. Contains Population Squares, VP values, Wormhole edges, and possibly Artifact or Discovery Tile symbols. Three rings: Inner (I), Middle (II), Outer (III).
Sector 001 at the center of the board. Defended by the Galactic Center Defense System. High VP value. Conquering it requires destroying the powerful GCDS.

A Structure built in a controlled sector requiring the Orbital tech. Costs 4 Materials. Provides an additional Population Square that can receive a Science or Money cube. Permanent and indestructible.

A Structure built in a controlled sector requiring the Monolith tech. Costs 10 Materials. Worth 3 VP at game end if built in a sector you still Control. Permanent and indestructible. Maximum one per sector.

Non-player ships found on newly explored sectors. Must be destroyed in combat to claim the sector's Discovery Tile. Use the Ancient Blueprint Tile for their stats. Destroyed Ancients earn 1 Reputation Tile draw each.
Powerful non-player ships defending Guardian Sectors. Stronger than Ancients. Destroyed Guardians earn 2 Reputation Tile draws each. Guardian Sectors have valuable Population Squares.

The powerful automated defense system protecting the Galactic Center Sector. Pins all ships in its sector. Destroying it earns 3 Reputation Tile draws. One of the most challenging battles in the game.

An alien species starting with Gauss Shield, Fusion Drive, and Plasma Cannon techs but 2 fewer Influence Discs. Draws 2 random Reputation Tiles during setup. A combat-oriented species with a tight economy.
An alien species with Advanced Labs starting tech and high Science production. Gets 2 Research Activations per Research Action. Excels at deep tech strategies and VP from tech track progress.

An alien species with 2 Explore Activations, 4 Colony Ships, and the Starbase tech. Earns 1 extra VP per controlled sector. Population Cubes are automatically destroyed by opponents at end of Combat Phase. Reduced Initiative but extra Computers on ship blueprints.

An alien species that can coexist with Ancients — they may have ships and Influence Discs in Ancient sectors but cannot battle them. Earns 1 VP per Ancient on the game board at end of game. Explore ability lets them flip 2 sectors and choose one to place.

An alien species with 3 Upgrade Activations per Upgrade Action and reduced building costs (Interceptor 2, Cruiser 4, Dreadnought 7, Starbase 2, Orbital 3, Monolith 8). Starts with the Positron Computer tech. Military-industrial powerhouse.

An alien species starting with a Cruiser instead of an Interceptor, plus Neutron Bombs and Gauss Shield techs. All ship blueprints have increased Initiative Bonuses. Extra energy on some blueprints. Built for early military aggression.

The human species with six faction variants (Directorate, Federation, Union, Republic, Conglomerate, Alliance), each with its own Starting Sector and slightly different starting Techs (all start with Starbase). Balanced and adaptable.
A Military tech that automatically destroys all Population Cubes in a sector when Attacking Population, instead of rolling dice. Does not affect Planta (whose cubes are already auto-destroyed).
A Nano tech that allows you to Explore, Move to, and Influence adjacent sectors even if only one of the two edges contains a Wormhole. Cannot be used to form Diplomatic Relations.
A Nano tech granting 1 extra Build Activation during each Build Action. Effectively gives you 3 builds per action instead of 2 (for most species).
A Nano tech that immediately gives you 1 additional Influence Disc placed on your Influence Track. Reduces upkeep pressure and enables more actions.
A Grid tech that immediately gives you 2 additional Influence Discs placed on your Influence Track. Major economy booster for action-heavy strategies.
A Military tech allowing you to place Population Cubes on Advanced Materials Population Squares using Colony Ships. Opens high-value materials production.
A Nano tech allowing you to place Population Cubes on Advanced Science Population Squares using Colony Ships. Opens high-value science production.
A Grid tech allowing you to place Population Cubes on Advanced Money Population Squares using Colony Ships. Opens high-value money production.
A Nano tech granting 5 Resources of a single type for each Artifact symbol on sectors you Control. An instant windfall when you control artifact-rich sectors.
A Rare Tech requiring opponents to use 2 ships to pin each of your ships. Dramatically increases fleet mobility in contested sectors.
A Rare Tech granting 1 additional Move Activation during each Move Action. Lets you reposition more ships per action for strategic flexibility.
A Rare Tech granting 2 additional Upgrade Activations during each Upgrade Action. Enables rapid fleet customization.
A Rare Tech that immediately lets you draw and resolve one Discovery Tile when researched. A free bonus reward.
A Rare Tech allowing you to place Population Cubes on any Advanced Population Squares with Colony Ships, regardless of type. The most powerful colonization tech in the game.
A Rare Tech that makes enemy Neutron Bombs have no effect on your Population Cubes. Does not affect Planta's species weakness.
A Rare Tech allowing you to freely split damage from Antimatter Cannons across multiple targets. Makes Antimatter Cannons devastating against fleets of small ships.
A Rare Tech that places a Warp Portal Tile on any sector you Control, connecting it to all other Warp Portal sectors. Worth 1 VP if controlled at game end. Enables cross-board movement.

The Money cost you must pay each Upkeep Phase, shown by the highest exposed number on your Influence Track. Increases as you take more actions and control more sectors. Failure to pay forces Trading or Abandoning sectors.

If you cannot pay Civilization Upkeep Cost after Trading and Abandoning sectors, your civilization is eliminated from the game. An extreme but rare outcome. Always monitor your Money Income vs. Upkeep Cost.
During Upkeep, gain Materials and Science equal to the highest exposed numbers on those Population Tracks. More Population Cubes on sectors = higher exposed numbers = more production.

An agreement between two players (4+ player games) formed by exchanging Ambassador Tiles containing Population Cubes. Requires Wormhole Connection, no ships in each other's sectors, and no Traitor Tile. Worth 1 VP each and boost production.
Having your ships in a sector where another player has ships or Control at the end of your action, breaking Diplomatic Relations. The aggressor receives the Traitor Tile (-2 VP).

A Population Square with a star symbol representing a harsh world. Requires a specific Advanced Tech (Advanced Mining, Advanced Labs, Advanced Economy, or Metasynthesis) to colonize. Gray advanced squares require the tech for any track being used.
A Population Square that can receive a cube from any Population Track (Materials, Science, or Money). Flexible and valuable for balancing your economy.
An optional module adding special sectors and a Discovery Tile. All Warp Portal sectors are considered adjacent with full Wormhole Connections. Enables cross-board movement and influence.
The area on your Control Board where destroyed Population Cubes are placed. Cubes return from the Graveyard to their Population Tracks during the Cleanup Phase.
Tracks the current round (1-8) on the Tech Tray. Advanced one step during each Cleanup Phase. The game ends after Round 8.

Your civilization's main board showing ship blueprints, tech tracks, action track, influence track, reputation track, starting stats, trade value, and species-specific abilities. Double-sided with a different species on each side.
A double-sided reference tile. Action Phase Overview on one side, Reactions Overview on the other. Flipped when you Pass to signal you can only take Reactions.
Holds your Population Tracks (Materials, Science, Money), Storage Track, Storage Markers, and Graveyards. Assembled from two pieces stacked on your Species Tray lid.
Central tray holding available Tech Tiles organized by category and cost. Refilled during each Cleanup Phase. Also holds the Round Marker track.
